description: Wire-format structural validation for PAT bearer tokens (ADR 0016). The format is `pat_<32hex-id>_<base64url-secret>` — a `pat_` prefix, exactly 32 lowercase hex characters for the id segment, an underscore separator, then a non-empty RFC 4648 §5 base64url-encoded secret with no padding. Every conforming SDK MUST classify these candidate strings identically when their `verify_pat_token` (or equivalent) starts with structural decoding. Tokens that pass structural validation may still fail at the lookup / secret-verify step; tokens that fail structural validation MUST short-circuit to `InvalidPatTokenError` (or the language equivalent) without any DB hit — both for performance and for the timing-oracle guarantee.

tests.description: Minimum well-formed shape: pat_ + 32 hex + _ + 1 base64url char. Structural validation MUST accept (the secret will fail Argon2id verify, but that is the lookup step's concern, not structure).
Typical 32-byte secret: 43-char base64url (32 bytes × 8 bits / 6 bits per char = 42.67 → 43 chars no padding).

A token with the share-token prefix MUST NOT be classified as a PAT — even though the same SDK may also handle share tokens. Prefix-routing is exclusive (per ADR 0016 §"Bearer routing"); cross-routing is a security defect.
A bare hex string with no prefix is structurally invalid — PATs are always prefixed.
The id segment MUST be lowercase hex. Uppercase or mixed case is structurally invalid; the Flametrench wire-format spec (docs/ids.md) is normative on lowercase.
Id segment shorter than 32 hex chars is structurally invalid.
Id segment longer than 32 hex chars is structurally invalid (the underscore at position 36 is the contract).
No underscore separator between id and secret — structurally invalid.
Trailing separator with no secret — structurally invalid.
Non-hex characters in the id segment — structurally invalid.
